title: Cystic Fibrosis — Overview
oneLiner: Autosomal recessive multisystem disease causing thick secretions, chronic lung infection, and pancreatic insufficiency; progressive lung disease is leading cause of death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax** – look for tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, sudden pleuritic pain, hypotension; immediate decompression needed.  
- **Massive haemoptysis** – brisk bleeding >300 mL/24 h or any haemoptysis with hypotension; call senior/ICU, consider bronchial artery embolisation.  
- **Acute respiratory failure** – SpO₂ <90% on usual oxygen, rising PaCO₂, altered GCS; escalate to high-flow O₂ or NIV early.  
- **Anaphylaxis to IV antibiotic** – rare but possible (especially aztreonam, ceftazidime); suspect if hypotension, wheeze, urticaria during infusion.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Chronic productive cough (muco‑purulent sputum)  
  - Dyspnoea (worse with exacerbation)  
  - Steatorrhoea, greasy/foul‑smelling stools (pancreatic insufficiency)  
  - Weight loss or failure to thrive (children)  
  - Sinus congestion, nasal polyps  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Bilaterally reduced breath sounds, crackles, wheeze  
  - Digital clubbing (common but non‑specific)  
  - Distended abdomen (DIOS)  
  - FTT in paediatric cases (low weight/height centiles)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Bronchiectasis (other causes)** – similar cough and sputum; differentiate by sweat chloride >60 mmol/L or CFTR gene mutation.  
- **Asthma** – reversible airflow obstruction, no steatorrhoea, normal sweat test.  
- **Primary ciliary dyskinesia** – situs inversus? normal sweat chloride, low nasal nitric oxide.  
- **Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)** – eosinophilia, high IgE, positive Aspergillus skin test; can co‑exist with CF.  
- **Splitter:** sweat chloride testing – >60 mmol/L confirms CF; intermediate (40–60) needs CFTR genetics.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Spirometry (FEV₁, FVC) – baseline and trend  
  - Oxygen saturation (target ≥92% if stable, >88% if chronic respiratory failure)  
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of child‑bearing age before any radiology)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC + CRP (infection marker)  
  - LFTs, U&E, glucose (monitor CF‑related liver disease, diabetes)  
  - Immunoglobulins (including IgG – low total may suggest need for Ig replacement)  
  - Aspergillus serology (IgE, precipitins) if ABPA suspected  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First‑line: **CXR** (hyperinflation, peribronchial thickening, pneumothorax?)  
  - Second‑line: **CT chest** (high‑resolution – cystic bronchiectasis, mucous plugging)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Sputum culture + sensitivity** – key pathogen: *P. aeruginosa*, *S. aureus* (including MRSA), *B. cepacia*, *H. influenzae*  
  - **Sweat chloride** (Gold standard) – ≥60 mmol/L = CF; “borderline” (40–59) requires CFTR mutation analysis  
  - **CFTR genetics** (if sweat test non‑diagnostic, newborn screening)  
- **Never‑miss test:** **Sweat chloride / CFTR genetics** in any adult with unexplained bronchiectasis – missing the diagnosis delays life‑saving treatment.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Oxygen to SpO₂ ≥92% (or patient’s baseline if chronically lower)  
  - Tension pneumothorax – needle decompression then chest drain  
  - Massive haemoptysis – call senior/ICU, cross‑match blood, hold anticoagulants  
  - IV antibiotics – **empiric anti‑pseudomonal** (e.g., ceftazidime 2 g IV TDS* + tobramycin [check local policy] – check TDM)  
  - Saline nebulised (0.9% or hypertonic 3%/7%) – maintain airway clearance  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Airway clearance – physiotherapy twice daily (PEP, oscillatory devices)  
  - Pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon®) with every meal/snack – dose per BNF (usual 500–2500 U/kg per meal)  
  - Nutritional support – high‑calorie, high‑protein diet ± nasogastric tube if anorexic  
  - Monitor sputum culture results, adjust antibiotics accordingly  
  - CF‑related diabetes – check capillary glucose 4 times daily; start insulin if fasting >7 mmol/L or random >11.1  
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - CFTR modulator therapy (e.g., ivacaftor, tezacaftor‑ivacaftor, elexacaftor‑tezacaftor‑ivacaftor) – for eligible genotypes (check local policy)  
  - Regular sputum surveillance (every 3 months)  
  - Annual assessment: FEV₁, HbA₁c, LFTs, bone densitometry, vitamin D levels  
  - Eradication protocol for new *P. aeruginosa* (28 days oral ciprofloxacin + inhaled colistin/tobramycin – check BNF)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** 5% dextrose with 0.45% NaCl at maintenance rate (watch for diabetes) – or IV fluids per local policy  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Either LMWH (e.g., dalteparin 2500 units daily if <50 kg or 5000Units daily if ≥50 kg) or mechanical – check weight & renal function  
  - **Diet:** High‑calorie, high‑protein, regular meals and snacks; if NBM for procedure, maintain IV glucose and enzymes can be held briefly  
  - **Observations:** 4‑hourly baseline; if unwell → hourly NEWS2 (target <5)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents (acute exacerbation):**  
  - Ceftazidime 2 g IV TDS* (use BNF or local policy)  
  - Tobramycin IV (e.g., 10 mg/kg once daily, then TDM) – **must check levels** pre‑dose (trough <1 mg/L) – severe ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity risk  
  - Colistin IV (check local policy – loading dose, then maintenance)  
  - Azithromycin (long‑term anti‑inflammatory) – 250 mg PO three times weekly (not for acute)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Meropenem 2 g IV TDS (if ceftazidime allergy)  
  - Aztreonam IV (for penicillin‑allergic patients)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Aminoglycosides + loop diuretics → ↑ nephrotoxicity  
  - Azithromycin + statins → ↑ risk of rhabdomyolysis  
  - CFTR modulators (ivacaftor) – strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampicin) reduce efficacy  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Aminoglycosides, colistin, ceftazidime – dose reduction in renal impairment (CrCl <50 mL/min)  
  - Ivacaftor – dose reduce if moderate‑severe hepatic impairment (Child‑Pugh B/C)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Tobramycin** – giving a second dose before checking levels can cause catastrophic ototoxicity. Always request TDM before 2nd dose.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Pneumothorax  
  - Massive haemoptysis (bronchial artery erosion)  
  - Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) – colicky pain, palpable mass in RIF, vomiting; treat with Movicol® / gastrografin enema  
  - Acute CF‑related diabetes (hyperglycaemia)  
  - Drug‑induced AKI (aminoglycosides, colistin)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Chronic respiratory failure (FEV₁ < 30% → consider lung transplantation)  
  - CF‑related diabetes (most common extra‑pulmonary complication after pancreatic insufficiency)  
  - CF‑related liver disease (cirrhosis, portal hypertension)  
  - Osteoporosis (vitamin D deficiency, steroid use)  
  - Male infertility (absent vas deferens)
sections.Prognosis:
- Median survival ~50 years (UK registry 2022)  
- Progressive decline in FEV₁ (average ~2% per year)  
- Worst prognosis: chronic *B. cepacia* complex infection, low FEV₁, high number of exacerbations  
- Lung transplantation option (2‑year survival ~70–80%)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do screen for CF‑related diabetes annually from age 10** – HbA₁c alone may miss early diabetes; consider 2‑hour OGTT.  
- **Encourage airway clearance immediately after physiotherapy** (e.g., positive expiratory pressure device) – best done before meals to avoid vomiting.  
- **Refer early for lung transplant assessment when FEV₁ < 30% or rapid decline** – delay worsens outcomes.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t stop pancreatic enzymes when NBM** – give with sips of water; withholding causes steatorrhoea and weight loss.  
- **Don’t use beta‑blockers for haemoptysis** – they may reduce cardiac output and worsen oxygenation; refer to specialist for bronchial artery embolisation.  
- **Don’t use aminoglycosides without TDM** – daily monitoring mandatory; missed dose can cause deafness.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening dyspnoea or SpO₂ <90% on home oxygen  
  - Any haemoptysis (even small amounts – may herald larger bleed)  
  - Vomiting or inability to take pancreatic enzymes for >24 h (risk of DIOS)  
  - Weight loss >2 kg in a month  
- **Follow-up:** CF team within 2 weeks; sputum culture repeated at next visit.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or NEWS2 ≥3 after initial treatment  
  - SpO₂ <88% despite 60% FiO₂  
  - Respiratory rate >30/min  
  - Acute haemoptysis >100 mL or any haemoptysis with hypotension  
  - Tension pneumothorax – immediate decompression then call cardio‑respiratory team  
- **ICU / crash team:**  
  - GCS <13  
  - pH <7.25 (acute on chronic respiratory acidosis)  
  - Need for non‑invasive ventilation or intubation  
  - Persistent massive haemoptysis despite bronchial artery embolisation  

*Doses are illustrative; always check BNF / local formulary for exact doses and adjustments.*

rawMarkdown:
## Cystic Fibrosis — Overview  
**One-liner:** Autosomal recessive multisystem disease causing thick secretions, chronic lung infection, and pancreatic insufficiency; progressive lung disease is leading cause of death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?  
- **Tension pneumothorax** – look for tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, sudden pleuritic pain, hypotension; immediate decompression needed.  
- **Massive haemoptysis** – brisk bleeding >300 mL/24 h or any haemoptysis with hypotension; call senior/ICU, consider bronchial artery embolisation.  
- **Acute respiratory failure** – SpO₂ <90% on usual oxygen, rising PaCO₂, altered GCS; escalate to high-flow O₂ or NIV early.  
- **Anaphylaxis to IV antibiotic** – rare but possible (especially aztreonam, ceftazidime); suspect if hypotension, wheeze, urticaria during infusion.

### Presentation  
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Chronic productive cough (muco‑purulent sputum)  
  - Dyspnoea (worse with exacerbation)  
  - Steatorrhoea, greasy/foul‑smelling stools (pancreatic insufficiency)  
  - Weight loss or failure to thrive (children)  
  - Sinus congestion, nasal polyps  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Bilaterally reduced breath sounds, crackles, wheeze  
  - Digital clubbing (common but non‑specific)  
  - Distended abdomen (DIOS)  
  - FTT in paediatric cases (low weight/height centiles)  

### Differential Diagnosis  
- **Bronchiectasis (other causes)** – similar cough and sputum; differentiate by sweat chloride >60 mmol/L or CFTR gene mutation.  
- **Asthma** – reversible airflow obstruction, no steatorrhoea, normal sweat test.  
- **Primary ciliary dyskinesia** – situs inversus? normal sweat chloride, low nasal nitric oxide.  
- **Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)** – eosinophilia, high IgE, positive Aspergillus skin test; can co‑exist with CF.  
- **Splitter:** sweat chloride testing – >60 mmol/L confirms CF; intermediate (40–60) needs CFTR genetics.  

### Investigations  
- **Bedside:**  
  - Spirometry (FEV₁, FVC) – baseline and trend  
  - Oxygen saturation (target ≥92% if stable, >88% if chronic respiratory failure)  
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of child‑bearing age before any radiology)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC + CRP (infection marker)  
  - LFTs, U&E, glucose (monitor CF‑related liver disease, diabetes)  
  - Immunoglobulins (including IgG – low total may suggest need for Ig replacement)  
  - Aspergillus serology (IgE, precipitins) if ABPA suspected  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First‑line: **CXR** (hyperinflation, peribronchial thickening, pneumothorax?)  
  - Second‑line: **CT chest** (high‑resolution – cystic bronchiectasis, mucous plugging)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Sputum culture + sensitivity** – key pathogen: *P. aeruginosa*, *S. aureus* (including MRSA), *B. cepacia*, *H. influenzae*  
  - **Sweat chloride** (Gold standard) – ≥60 mmol/L = CF; “borderline” (40–59) requires CFTR mutation analysis  
  - **CFTR genetics** (if sweat test non‑diagnostic, newborn screening)  
- **Never‑miss test:** **Sweat chloride / CFTR genetics** in any adult with unexplained bronchiectasis – missing the diagnosis delays life‑saving treatment.

### Management  
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Oxygen to SpO₂ ≥92% (or patient’s baseline if chronically lower)  
  - Tension pneumothorax – needle decompression then chest drain  
  - Massive haemoptysis – call senior/ICU, cross‑match blood, hold anticoagulants  
  - IV antibiotics – **empiric anti‑pseudomonal** (e.g., ceftazidime 2 g IV TDS* + tobramycin [check local policy] – check TDM)  
  - Saline nebulised (0.9% or hypertonic 3%/7%) – maintain airway clearance  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Airway clearance – physiotherapy twice daily (PEP, oscillatory devices)  
  - Pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon®) with every meal/snack – dose per BNF (usual 500–2500 U/kg per meal)  
  - Nutritional support – high‑calorie, high‑protein diet ± nasogastric tube if anorexic  
  - Monitor sputum culture results, adjust antibiotics accordingly  
  - CF‑related diabetes – check capillary glucose 4 times daily; start insulin if fasting >7 mmol/L or random >11.1  
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - CFTR modulator therapy (e.g., ivacaftor, tezacaftor‑ivacaftor, elexacaftor‑tezacaftor‑ivacaftor) – for eligible genotypes (check local policy)  
  - Regular sputum surveillance (every 3 months)  
  - Annual assessment: FEV₁, HbA₁c, LFTs, bone densitometry, vitamin D levels  
  - Eradication protocol for new *P. aeruginosa* (28 days oral ciprofloxacin + inhaled colistin/tobramycin – check BNF)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** 5% dextrose with 0.45% NaCl at maintenance rate (watch for diabetes) – or IV fluids per local policy  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Either LMWH (e.g., dalteparin 2500 units daily if <50 kg or 5000Units daily if ≥50 kg) or mechanical – check weight & renal function  
  - **Diet:** High‑calorie, high‑protein, regular meals and snacks; if NBM for procedure, maintain IV glucose and enzymes can be held briefly  
  - **Observations:** 4‑hourly baseline; if unwell → hourly NEWS2 (target <5)

### Drugs & Doses  
- **First‑line agents (acute exacerbation):**  
  - Ceftazidime 2 g IV TDS* (use BNF or local policy)  
  - Tobramycin IV (e.g., 10 mg/kg once daily, then TDM) – **must check levels** pre‑dose (trough <1 mg/L) – severe ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity risk  
  - Colistin IV (check local policy – loading dose, then maintenance)  
  - Azithromycin (long‑term anti‑inflammatory) – 250 mg PO three times weekly (not for acute)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Meropenem 2 g IV TDS (if ceftazidime allergy)  
  - Aztreonam IV (for penicillin‑allergic patients)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Aminoglycosides + loop diuretics → ↑ nephrotoxicity  
  - Azithromycin + statins → ↑ risk of rhabdomyolysis  
  - CFTR modulators (ivacaftor) – strong CYP3A4 inducers (e.g., rifampicin) reduce efficacy  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Aminoglycosides, colistin, ceftazidime – dose reduction in renal impairment (CrCl <50 mL/min)  
  - Ivacaftor – dose reduce if moderate‑severe hepatic impairment (Child‑Pugh B/C)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Tobramycin** – giving a second dose before checking levels can cause catastrophic ototoxicity. Always request TDM before 2nd dose.

### Complications  
- **Acute:**  
  - Pneumothorax  
  - Massive haemoptysis (bronchial artery erosion)  
  - Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) – colicky pain, palpable mass in RIF, vomiting; treat with Movicol® / gastrografin enema  
  - Acute CF‑related diabetes (hyperglycaemia)  
  - Drug‑induced AKI (aminoglycosides, colistin)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Chronic respiratory failure (FEV₁ < 30% → consider lung transplantation)  
  - CF‑related diabetes (most common extra‑pulmonary complication after pancreatic insufficiency)  
  - CF‑related liver disease (cirrhosis, portal hypertension)  
  - Osteoporosis (vitamin D deficiency, steroid use)  
  - Male infertility (absent vas deferens)  

### Prognosis  
- Median survival ~50 years (UK registry 2022)  
- Progressive decline in FEV₁ (average ~2% per year)  
- Worst prognosis: chronic *B. cepacia* complex infection, low FEV₁, high number of exacerbations  
- Lung transplantation option (2‑year survival ~70–80%)  

### Pearls (positive actions)  
- **Do screen for CF‑related diabetes annually from age 10** – HbA₁c alone may miss early diabetes; consider 2‑hour OGTT.  
- **Encourage airway clearance immediately after physiotherapy** (e.g., positive expiratory pressure device) – best done before meals to avoid vomiting.  
- **Refer early for lung transplant assessment when FEV₁ < 30% or rapid decline** – delay worsens outcomes.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)  
- **Don’t stop pancreatic enzymes when NBM** – give with sips of water; withholding causes steatorrhoea and weight loss.  
- **Don’t use beta‑blockers for haemoptysis** – they may reduce cardiac output and worsen oxygenation; refer to specialist for bronchial artery embolisation.  
- **Don’t use aminoglycosides without TDM** – daily monitoring mandatory; missed dose can cause deafness.  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)  
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening dyspnoea or SpO₂ <90% on home oxygen  
  - Any haemoptysis (even small amounts – may herald larger bleed)  
  - Vomiting or inability to take pancreatic enzymes for >24 h (risk of DIOS)  
  - Weight loss >2 kg in a month  
- **Follow-up:** CF team within 2 weeks; sputum culture repeated at next visit.

### Escalation Criteria  
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or NEWS2 ≥3 after initial treatment  
  - SpO₂ <88% despite 60% FiO₂  
  - Respiratory rate >30/min  
  - Acute haemoptysis >100 mL or any haemoptysis with hypotension  
  - Tension pneumothorax – immediate decompression then call cardio‑respiratory team  
- **ICU / crash team:**  
  - GCS <13  
  - pH <7.25 (acute on chronic respiratory acidosis)  
  - Need for non‑invasive ventilation or intubation  
  - Persistent massive haemoptysis despite bronchial artery embolisation  

*Doses are illustrative; always check BNF / local formulary for exact doses and adjustments.*
